meta.title: Analyse an area of computer science
meta.purpose: This achievement standard involves analysing an area of computer science.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved-level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved-level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved-level indicators are met; no Merit-level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence-level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

omis.description: Explains key aspects of computer vision, such as image processing or feature extraction.

positive_signals: Explains at least one key computer vision concept such as noise, thresholding, edge detection, or image processing
Describes what the concept is or how it works
Uses the concept as part of a clear explanation rather than just naming it
negative_signals: Merely names a concept without explanation
Uses vague phrases such as 'computer vision uses images' with no key aspect
Lists several concepts without describing any of them
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often name a technical term and assume that counts as explanation, but this indicator requires a brief explanation of the concept.

success_examples: Noise refers to random variation in brightness or colour in images.
Edge detection helps identify object boundaries by finding brightness changes.

omis.description: Describes an algorithm or mechanism behind a computer vision technique.

positive_signals: Names a specific computer vision technique or algorithm
Explains how that technique operates
Describes the role of the mechanism in producing the computer vision result
negative_signals: Names an algorithm without describing how it works
Describes computer vision generally with no specific mechanism
Mentions a technique with no operational explanation
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often recognise the algorithm name, but this indicator requires an explanation of how the mechanism operates.

success_examples: Convolution applies a kernel that moves across the image to detect features.
Viola-Jones uses rectangular Haar-like features to detect faces.

omis.description: Provides at least one example of how computer vision is used or implemented.

positive_signals: Gives a specific real-world computer vision application
Links the application to a technique such as CNNs, stereo vision, or feature detection
Explains how the technique is used in that context
negative_signals: Mentions AI or automation without a computer vision application
Names a context with no technique link
Describes a general use with no visual-processing component
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often give a broad technology example, but this indicator requires a specific computer vision use linked to a technique.

success_examples: Stereo vision is used in autonomous cars to detect distances to obstacles.
Medical imaging uses CNNs to classify MRI scans.

omis.description: Identifies key problems or issues related to computer vision.

positive_signals: Identifies a specific issue such as noise, false positives, bias, or limited training data
Names the issue clearly enough to show technical understanding
Links the issue to the computer vision system being discussed
negative_signals: Uses vague statements such as 'it can be wrong sometimes'
Mentions problems generally without naming a specific issue
Describes a limitation with no clear issue statement
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often imply that computer vision has problems, but this indicator requires at least one clearly identified issue.

success_examples: Low-light conditions increase noise, making features harder to detect.
Biased training data can lead to inaccurate facial recognition.

omis.description: Explains how identified problems in computer vision have been or may be addressed.

positive_signals: Presents a mitigation method linked to a known computer vision issue
Explains how the method addresses the issue
Uses a specific response such as filtering, augmentation, or better training data
negative_signals: Names a tool or method without saying what issue it addresses
Identifies an issue but gives no response
Offers a generic fix with no purpose or linkage
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often know a mitigation method, but this indicator requires them to explain how it addresses the identified problem.

success_examples: Denoising filters smooth out random brightness variations before analysis.
Augmenting data with rotated images improves generalisation.

omis.description: Explains how technical capabilities and limitations relate to human needs or uses.

positive_signals: Links a technical capability or limitation to a human need, use, or experience
Explains the real-world effect on users or system reliability
Uses an example showing why the technical detail matters for people
negative_signals: Describes technical features with no human or use-related impact
Uses generic usefulness statements with no specific consequence
Mentions a user context without linking it to the technical capability or limitation
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often stay at the technical level, but Merit requires them to connect the capability or limitation to people, uses, or reliability.

success_examples: Facial recognition fails under poor lighting, reducing security reliability.
Lag in detection impacts user experience in AR systems.

omis.description: Compares and contrasts different perspectives on computer vision.

positive_signals: Contrasts at least two perspectives using comparative language
Explains how the perspectives differ rather than listing them separately
Uses wording such as 'whereas', 'unlike', or 'on the other hand'
negative_signals: Lists different perspectives without comparison
Describes two approaches with no contrast language
Focuses on only one perspective in detail
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often mention several viewpoints, but this indicator requires an explicit compare-and-contrast structure.

success_examples: LiDAR is accurate for depth, whereas stereo vision is cheaper and more scalable.
Manual image tagging is slow, but CNNs automate it faster with less accuracy.

omis.description: Draws insightful conclusions about computer vision.

positive_signals: Draws a conclusion that synthesizes earlier ideas
Shows forward-looking thought such as a prediction, implication, or improvement
Moves beyond repeating previously stated facts
negative_signals: Restates earlier points without synthesis
Ends with a summary only
Adds a future claim with no real connection to the analysis
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often write a summary paragraph, but Excellence requires a conclusion that synthesizes or extends the analysis.

success_examples: As CV improves, it may replace manual diagnostic steps in rural healthcare.
Techniques used in self-driving cars could support visually impaired navigation.

omis.description: Evaluates the broader impact of computer vision in society or technology.

positive_signals: Makes a judgment about the broader impact of computer vision
Supports that judgment with a rationale or example
Evaluates significance rather than merely listing pros and cons
negative_signals: Lists positives and negatives without evaluating them
Makes a judgment with no supporting rationale
Describes an impact with no evaluative statement
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often describe effects on society or technology, but this indicator requires a supported evaluative judgment.

success_examples: CV helps in security, but raises privacy concerns with constant surveillance.
Automated inspections improve efficiency but reduce skilled labour demand.

omis.description: Makes a justified prediction about the future of computer vision.

positive_signals: States a future prediction about computer vision
Explains why that future development is likely
Links the prediction to current techniques, trends, or constraints
negative_signals: Gives a speculative future claim with no reasoning
Repeats a hope or opinion instead of a justified prediction
Names a future application without explaining why it is plausible
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Students often make a future claim, but this indicator requires reasoning that justifies the prediction.

success_examples: With cheaper sensors, CV will expand into home robotics and elder care.
Advances in CNNs will make diagnosis faster in under-resourced clinics.
